retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sensory System -- Cellular Level -- Nerve Fiber Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Group I or A-alpha fibers: Ia fibers transmit proprioceptive input from primary endings of muscle spindles. Ib fibers transmit proprioceptive input from Golgi tendon organs. Group II or A-beta fibers: Transmit proprioceptive input from secondary endings of muscle spindles and touch and pressure input from specialized receptors in the skin and deep tissues (Merkel discs, Ruffini endings, Meissner corpuscles, and Pacinian corpuscles). Group III or A-delta fibers: Transmit “fast” pain, cold, pressure, and touch from the skin and visceral afferents. Group IV or C fibers: Transmit “slow” pain, heat, and pressure from the skin, muscle, and visceral afferents. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Vibratory Sense -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Our somatosensory system has three basic types of sensory receptors that detect different types of external stimuli. These include mechanoreceptors that detect light touch, vibration, pressure, and texture; nociceptors that detect pain; and thermoreceptors that detect temperature. Mechanoreceptors further divide into Merkel disks, Meissner’s corpuscles, Ruffini endings, and Pacinian corpuscles based on the specific type of mechanical stimuli they perceive. The perception of vibratory sensation is by two main types of mechanoreceptors, Meissner corpuscles (MC) and Pacinian corpuscles (PC). MCs are large myelinated fibers that detect low-frequency vibration and are present in glabrous (smooth, hairless) skin on fingertips and eyelids. PCs contain large myelinated A-beta fibers which are rapidly adapting and are present in the deeper layers of skin, ligaments, and joints where their function is to detect high-frequency vibration and deep pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Sensory Nerves -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Sensory nerves have different types of nerves fibers depending on their associated receptors. Classification of sensory nerves includes the numerical or Erlanger and Gasser system. [1] Proprioceptors (position sensors) receive innervation via type Ia (A-alpha: muscle spindle), Ib (A-alpha: Golgi tendon organ), and II (A-beta: touch and pressure) sensory fibers. These fibers are large and myelinated with rapid conduction velocities. Mechanoreceptor innervation is by type II and III (A-delta: free nerve endings, cold) sensory fibers. Nociceptors (pain sensors) and thermoreceptor innervation by type III and IV (C: slow pain, heat) fibers. A-delta fibers are thinly myelinated and transmit information primarily related to acute pain to facilitate a withdrawal reflex upon synapse in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord. C fibers are smaller, unmyelinated fibers that require a higher threshold of stimulus than A-delta fibers. These are responsible for the slower onset of deeper pain after an initial insult relayed by the faster A-delta fibers. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sensory System -- Cellular Level -- Nerve Fiber Classification
retrieved_docs.content: The nerve fiber types along which action potentials propagate are classified according to diameter and conduction velocity, with an Erlanger Gasser classification (A-C) as well as a numerical classification (I-IV). From A (largest, myelinated, fastest) to C (smallest, unmyelinated, slowest), nerve fiber diameter decreases, as does relative conduction velocity.

retrieved_docs.title: Sensory Neuropathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Appropriate treatment and management are determined by sensory neuropathy classification, which is based on nerve size and degree of myelination. When assessing small fiber neuropathies, the small somatic Aδ and small unmyelinated C fibers are of particular interest. These fibers transmit noxious and thermal signals and regulate preganglionic sympathetic and parasympathetic function via the Aδ fibers and postganglionic autonomic function via the C fibers. [1] Disruption of the normal physiological state of these fibers typically results in burning and shooting pain with paresthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Stereognosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The first-order neurons of the DCMLT have cell bodies in dorsal root ganglia with peripheral processes extending out to sensory receptors (Pacinian corpuscles, Merkel cells, Golgi tendon organs, and muscle spindles) and central processes that enter the spinal cord in the large sensory fiber entry zone of the dorsal horn ascending ipsilaterally in the dorsal columns. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Unmyelinated Nerve Fibers -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ability to perceive accurate and specific afferent sensory information from the external world is vital to human function, and a range of peripheral nerve fibers perform this role. These specialized peripheral nerve fibers are classified into 4 subclasses according to their fiber diameter, conduction velocity, and extent of myelination. C-type fibers are unmyelinated fibers that form 1 of these groups, and they are involved in the afferent transfer of temperature, burning pain, and itch from the periphery to synapse, principally in lamina I and II of the dorsal spinal horn.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Nerve -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The sensory receptors are specialized regions or structures of the sensory neurons responsible for detecting all senses and performing many functions throughout the body. These receptors respond to changes in the environment by converting chemical or mechanical energy into action potentials to be transmitted towards the central nervous system. Different types of stimulus receptors mediate each of the five basic human senses (sight, hearing, smell, taste, and touch). There are many other types of receptors, including chemoreceptors, photoreceptors, baroreceptors, and proprioceptors, which detect chemicals, light, pressure, and movement, respectively. Nerve fibers have been categorized based on numerous parameters, including relative diameter and myelination, as outlined in the image below. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Touch Receptor -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: There are two major types of mammalian skin; glabrous (non-hairy) and hairy skin. Glabrous skin is specific for discriminative touch and is present in the hands and feet. This skin gives proper grip control and can determine texture and shape. Hairy skin covers the other 90% of our body and is closely related to both pleasurable sensations, like stroking, and pain, such as hair pulling. [3] Four mechanosensory end organs are found in glabrous skin: Ruffini endings, Meissner corpuscles, Pacinian corpuscles, and Merkel discs. Both Meissner corpuscles and Pacinian corpuscles are rapidly adapting while Merkel cells and Ruffini endings are slowly adapting nerve fibers. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sensory System -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Different receptor types and free nerve endings, each with their own specific function, generate the overall sense of touch, position in space, and pain. For the purposes of this article, “mechanoreceptor” refers to the mechanosensory end-organ structure and its associated low-threshold mechanoreceptor (LTMR). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Ciguatera Toxicity -- Introduction -- Nerve Fiber Classification
retrieved_docs.content: Group II fibers innervate cutaneous tactile receptors and the muscle spindles' flower-spray endings. These fibers are either type Aβ or Aγ. Group III fibers are of the Aδ type, carrying the sensations of temperature, crude touch, and pricking pain (fast pain). Group IV fibers are type C fibers that transmit "slow pain" (ie, visceral pain), itch, temperature, and crude touch sensations.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Mechanoreceptors -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: For tactile receptors used for touch, there is precise coding of mechanical information. Mechanoreceptors will respond to vibrations, indentations of the skin, or movement of hair follicles. [2] Cutaneous mechanoreceptors localize in different layers of the skin with varying ranges of mechanical stimuli detection. This range sensing divides into two groups: low-threshold mechanoreceptors (LTMRs) and high-threshold mechanoreceptors (HTMRs). LTMRs react to light, benign pressure, while HTMRs react to stronger, harmful, mechanical pressure. Both mechanoreceptor types reside in DRGs and cranial sensory ganglia. Nerves associated with these two mechanoreceptor types classify as A-beta-, A-delta- or C-fibers based on their action potential conduction velocities. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Mechanoreceptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanoreceptors are a type of somatosensory receptors which relay extracellular stimulus to intracellular signal transduction through mechanically gated ion channels. The external stimuli are usually in the form of touch, pressure, stretching, sound waves, and motion. Mechanoreceptors are present in the superficial as well as the deeper layer of skin and near bone. These receptors are either encapsulated or unencapsulated, and the free nerve endings are usually unencapsulated dendrite. There are four major categories of tactile mechanoreceptors: Merkel’s disks, Meissner’s corpuscles, Ruffini endings, and Pacinian corpuscles. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Sensory Receptors -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Sensory receptors occur in specialized organs such as the eyes, ears, nose, and mouth, as well as internal organs. Each receptor type conveys a distinct sensory modality to integrate into a single perceptual frame eventually. This information is achieved by the conversion of energy into an electrical signal by specialized mechanisms. In this report, we will discuss a basic overview of sensory systems, focusing on sensory receptors.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Unmyelinated Nerve Fibers -- Structure and Function -- Categories of Unmyelinated Nerve Fibers
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple studies review the classification of C mechanoreceptors based on receptive and reflex properties of axonal conduction latency, slowing to repetitive electrical stimulation. One study classified C-mechanoreceptors into C tactile afferents (CT) and C-mechanosensitive nociceptors (CM). CT has less latency slowing than CM due to ionic channel (NaV) differences. Another study categorized C-mechanoreceptors into 3 main classes of C fibers. First, efferent sympathetic fibers (Symp). Next are afferent mechano-responsive fibers (CM) responsive to both mechanical and heat pain stimuli thresholds and thermal pain's temporal and spatial resolution. These fibers exhibit axonal conduction latency slowing during repetitive electrical stimulation of the skin. The third class, the afferent mechano-insensitive fibers (CMi), is not responsive to mechanical and heat but responsible for neurogenic inflammation (eg, hyperalgesia) with axon reflex flare. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Stereognosis -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Nerve fibers introduced to the dorsal columns from the lower extremities localize to the medial aspect as they ascend ipsilaterally, forming bundles of axons called the fasciculi gracilis. Similar proprioceptive and discriminative touch information from the upper extremities (spinal level T5 and above) localizes to adjacent lateral axon bundles called the fasciculi cuneatus. The fact that nerve fibers enter the spinal cord and add to the dorsal column fascicles in a medial-to-lateral manner is an important concept. This somatotopic organization of the ascending tracts allows for precise localization of these stimuli when they eventually reach the primary somatosensory cortex of the parietal lobe. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2186
retrieved_docs.content: Headache, including migraine, is classified as a condition that falls under the broader category of nervous system and sense organ diseases. This hierarchical relationship indicates that headaches and migraines are considered manifestations of disorders affecting the nervous system and sensory organs, highlighting their relevance within the spectrum of neurological and sensory pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 831
retrieved_docs.content: central role of peripheral nerves in the pathophysiology of diverse neurologic disorders and underscore the need for precise classification, thorough neurologic evaluation, and targeted management to guide diagnosis, treatment, and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: The Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC)‑classification system (SRC atoms) is categorized as a type of sensory organ drugs, indicating that this segment of the ATC coding scheme is specifically organized under the class of medications that act on sensory organs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: pain (ألم), phantom‑limb syndrome (متلازمة الطرف الشبحي), sensory deficits, motor weakness, and paralysis (شلل). This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 1274
retrieved_docs.content: organized under nervous system and sense organ diseases, which themselves are subsumed by the overarching concept of pain. Specific nerve injuries and peripheral neuropathy are linked to pain, and pain is further connected to complex syndromes such as phantom limb syndrome, underscoring the central role of pain in the clinical understanding and management of these interconnected medical entities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1301
retrieved_docs.content: OtoLogicals constitute a broad class of sensory organ drugs, and within this overarching category, sensory organ drugs are recognized as a specific subclass. This relationship indicates that all sensory organ drugs are encompassed by the larger group of OtoLogicals, highlighting the hierarchical organization of pharmacologic agents that target sensory systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 269
retrieved_docs.content: concepts (pain, paralysis) with anatomical sites (peripheral nerves, specific cranial and spinal nerves, tendon sheaths, synovium), disease entities (headache, ocular disorders, the extensive list of neuropathies, muscular disorders, entrapment syndromes, rare hereditary and inflammatory conditions), and procedural interventions (nerve incision, decompression), providing clinicians with a comprehensive taxonomy for precise diagnosis, coding, and patient‑centered management of nervous‑system and sense‑organ disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 264
retrieved_docs.content: strategies in the management of neuromuscular, neuro‑vascular and entrapment neuropathies, supporting precise diagnosis, coding and coordinated patient care across overlapping neurologic, sensory and musculoskeletal domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: tunnel decompression** and decompression of the **tarsal/ankle‑tarsal tunnel**, underscoring the therapeutic relevance of surgical relief of entrapment. All listed conditions carry a **Neurologic** qualifier, reflecting their placement within a structured diagnostic and management hierarchy that integrates pain, sensory and motor dysfunction, inflammatory and toxic neuropathies, entrapment syndromes, and targeted surgical procedures for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of nerve is classified as a type of peripheral nerve decompression, indicating that it falls under the broader category of decompression procedures targeting peripheral nerves.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1516
retrieved_docs.content: OPHTHALMOLOGICAL AND OTOLOGICAL PREPARATIONS are classified as SENSORY ORGAN DRUGS, indicating that these medications are intended for the treatment of sensory organs such as the eyes and ears. As sensory organ drugs, they are used to manage conditions affecting vision and hearing, and their categorization helps guide appropriate therapeutic use and regulatory classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 86
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base groups a wide array of neurologic and neuromuscular conditions under the umbrella of **Other disorders of the nervous system**, emphasizing their inter‑relationships with pain, paralysis, and sensory disturbance. Generic pain classifications (including “Pain, not elsewhere classified”) and specific pain entities such as **trigeminal nerve pain** and **facial nerve disorders** (including **facial paralysis** and upper‑facial atrophy) are linked to peripheral nerve pathology. Lesions of individual peripheral nerves—**plantar nerve**, **calcaneal (tibial) nerve**, **sciatic nerve**, **thoracic peripheral nerves**, and hemorrhage into a nerve trunk—are each associated with localized motor loss, foot deformities, or broader paralytic outcomes. **Peripheral neuropathy** is a central subcategory connected to pain and paralysis and subdivided into numerous etiologies: **alcoholic polyneuropathy**, **diabetic neuropathy**, **idiopathic progressive neuropathy**,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Headache; including migraine is classified as a condition that belongs to the broader category of Nervous system and sense organ diseases. The category Nervous system and sense organ diseases also includes the child entity اضطراب العين (eye disorder). Recognizing that both headache—including migraine—and eye disorders are subtypes of Nervous system and sense organ diseases helps clinicians organize related symptoms, consider shared pathophysiological mechanisms, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 184
retrieved_docs.content: nerves, demonstrating that systemic inflammatory processes contribute to peripheral neuropathy. Therapeutic **decompression of the carpal tunnel** is related to the occurrence of **tarsal tunnel syndrome**, highlighting the interplay between compressive neuropathies of the wrist and ankle. Overall, the community’s multilingual, hierarchical network integrates symptom‑based categories (pain, paralysis, gait disorder), disease entities, anatomical sites, and procedural interventions to support comprehensive coding, diagnosis, and management of diverse neurological and sensory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Acetylcholine is recognized as having a broader relationship with neurotransmitery, indicating that it functions as a neurotransmitter within the nervous system. This classification underscores acetylcholine’s role in transmitting signals between nerve cells and across neuromuscular junctions, which is fundamental to processes such as muscle contraction, autonomic nervous system regulation, and cognitive functions. Understanding acetylcholine’s position as a neurotransmitter is essential for comprehending its impact on both normal physiological signaling and various clinical conditions where its pathways may be altered.
